Hi Hugo, that's helpful context — thank you.

What you're describing sounds like solid practice: reviewing client legal requirements before committing to a project. That protects you contractually.
The documentation question I'm raising is one level deeper — it's about what's inside the video itself. Which tools generated it. Whether any real person's likeness appears. Whether the training data is cleared for commercial use. That assessment isn't covered by a client's terms and conditions — it's about the content's own provenance.

The IAB released a framework in January requiring agencies to assess and document exactly this before campaigns run. It's industry standard, not law — but it's what holding group compliance teams are now using as the benchmark. The EU AI Act transparency requirements also hit in August.

If your agency network clients are already asking for this level of content documentation and you have a system for producing it, you're ahead of most. I'd be curious whether that's part of your current workflow or whether it's something you've navigated informally.

Happy to share a sample if useful.11:19 pm

Tue Apr 07 2026

Hi Hugo, that makes sense — knowing which models the client permits before you take the project is good intake discipline.

The question that's coming up at delivery is slightly different: when the brand's compliance team or E&O insurer reviews the final video, they're asking for a document that proves the approved models were actually used in that specific piece of content. Not the pre-project approval — the Chain of Title for the deliverable itself.
That's the document most agencies don't have at handoff, even when the intake process was clean. Here's a sample example of our Chain of Title.

Is this something that clients have asked from you? Or, do you deliver this information in a different way or format?

Hi Hugo,

Happy to break down both options we offer:

1. Creator Record — $29: Self-attested. The creator or agency fills in the disclosures directly — tools used, account confirmation, creative process brief.

Automated PDF delivered instantly with a self-attested stamp. Good for internal records or lower-stakes deliverables where the client isn't asking for third-party sign-off.

2. SI8 Certified — $499: Human review. We independently verify the content against what was submitted and produce the Chain of Title document with our verification stamp.
For SI8 Certified, here's what we need from you:
- commercial plan receipts or account screenshots for each AI tool used
(this is the core of the commercial use authorization)
- a written description of the creative process covering the prompts developed, iteration decisions, editorial choices, and any post-generation editing
- confirmation of whether any real person's likeness or voice appears and if so signed releases covering commercial use and AI derivatives
- confirmation of whether any copyrighted characters or branded IP appears and if so written licenses from the rights holders - audio and music disclosure — source of all audio in the piece, whether AI-generated or licensed, with documentation if licensed
- the final video file or a secure link for review.

If your existing process is already thorough, Creator Record formalizes it cheaply.

If your clients are asking for third-party sign-off, SI8 Certified is the document you hand them.

Curious which situation fits most of your projects — happy to run through a specific example if that's easier.

Thanks!

Hi Hugo, that's helpful context — thank you.

What you're describing sounds like solid practice: reviewing client legal requirements before committing to a project. That protects you contractually.
The documentation question I'm raising is one level deeper — it's about what's inside the video itself. Which tools generated it. Whether any real person's likeness appears. Whether the training data is cleared for commercial use. That assessment isn't covered by a client's terms and conditions — it's about the content's own provenance.

The IAB released a framework in January requiring agencies to assess and document exactly this before campaigns run. It's industry standard, not law — but it's what holding group compliance teams are now using as the benchmark. The EU AI Act transparency requirements also hit in August.

If your agency network clients are already asking for this level of content documentation and you have a system for producing it, you're ahead of most. I'd be curious whether that's part of your current workflow or whether it's something you've navigated informally.

Happy to share a sample if useful.11:19 pm

Hi Hugo, that makes sense — knowing which models the client permits before you take the project is good intake discipline.

The question that's coming up at delivery is slightly different: when the brand's compliance team or E&O insurer reviews the final video, they're asking for a document that proves the approved models were actually used in that specific piece of content. Not the pre-project approval — the Chain of Title for the deliverable itself.
That's the document most agencies don't have at handoff, even when the intake process was clean. Here's a sample example of our Chain of Title.

Hi Ruth,

Quick question. When you deliver AI-generated video to a client for campaign approval, are their legal teams asking for documentation on how it was made?

Seeing this come up more this year.04:12 pm
Hi Ivy,
In some of our projects, we do use AI tools, but in many cases these are run locally on our own machines. This means the outputs are fully controlled on our end, and the ownership remains clearly defined between us and the client.

For certain animation models and tools, we work under licensed packages that explicitly grant commercial usage rights. We make sure everything we use is properly covered.

If a client’s legal team needs reassurance, we’re able to provide supporting documentation, including invoices and licensing details that confirm when and how the models or tools were used, along with their usage rights.

So far, this approach has worked smoothly and we haven’t run into any issues from a legal or approval standpoint.

Hey Ivy,from my experience, the documentation requirements usually fall into two categories depending on the production model. If I’m generating assets manually, I don’t typically provide per video documentation. The guardrail here is that I only use a specific stack of AI tools already vetted and approved by their legal team for commercial use.

This includes documenting the entire tech stack, demoing the internal workflows, and explaining how the engine operates. Hi Leimi,

Quick question. When you deliver AI-generated video to a client for campaign approval, are their legal teams asking for documentation on how it was made?

Seeing this come up more this year.03:26 pm
Hey Vanessa,
I’ve never been asked for documentation on my process. Why do you ask? Just curious03:27 pm
Fri Apr 03 2026

Hey Leimi, good question — and the honest answer is it's very new.

Most people haven't been asked yet. The shift is coming from a few directions at once. The IAB released its first AI Transparency and Disclosure Framework in January, requiring agencies to assess and document AI video before campaigns run.

The ASA banned a Facebook ad in March for using AI video that misrepresented a product. EU AI Act transparency requirements hit in August.

Brand legal teams at the holding groups are starting to catch up. The request for documentation is likely to become standard this year — not because regulators are knocking on doors yet, but because agencies are getting ahead of it.
What we do is produce the Chain of Title document that covers all of it: which tools were used, whether any real person appears, IP and training data status, brand safety assessment. It's the paper trail for when a legal team asks.

Worth a quick look? Happy to send a sample.

Thanks,
Vanessa11:21 pm
